IN THE HIGH COURT OF LESOTHO
(COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
HELD AT MASERU                                                         CCT/643/2020
In the matter between –
MPHOU KHAFA                                                              APPLICANT
And
SECHABA SEPAMO                                                        1
ST
RESPONDENT
‘MALIAHELO QHOBELA                                              2
ND
RESPONDENT
Neutral Citation
: Mphou Khafa v. Sechaba Sepamo and Another
[2026] LSHC 19
Comm. (3
rd
February 2026)
CORAM:  M. S. KOPO, J
HEARD:  20
TH
AUGUST, 17
TH
, AND 30
TH
SEPTEMBER, 2025
DELIVERED: 03
RD
FEBRUARY 2026
SUMMARY
Law of Contract – the surrounding circumstances are to be considered in determining if there is a contract –
Held
– All the surrounding circumstance point to the fact that there was a contract between the parties.
Annotations
Cases
Lesotho
Lesotho National General Insurance v Rhythm and Steal (Pty) Ltd
(CCA/0018/2022)
[2023] LSHC 3
(21 March 2023)
Maphaong v Minister of Education (CIV/APN 155 of 2020)
[2021] LSHC 42
(22 April 2021)
Pegasus Supplies (Pty) Ltd v Lirahalibonoe (Pty) Ltd (CIV/APN/253/2021)
[2022] LSHC 88
(16 March 2022)
South Africa
Allen v Sixteen Stirling Investment (Pty) Ltd1974 (4) SA 164
Statutes
Deeds Registry
Act 12 of 1967
Legal Aid
Act No. 19 of 1978
JUDGMENT
[A] INTRODUCTION
Most of the time a dispute that drives people into adversaries starts as a noble idea that is meant to benefit such people. The present matter emanates from a noble idea between a niece and her uncle that was meant to be for the benefit of both of them. It culminated into a contract or a disputed contract between them and a third party. The dispute emanating from that contract or disputed contract is the subject of this matter.
[B] BACKGROUND
The Respondents are relatives. The 1
st
Respondent, Mr. Sechaba Sepamo, is the uncle of the 2
nd
Respondent, Mrs. ‘Malihaelo Qhobela (Nee Limpho Sepamo). The 2
nd
Respondent is the daughter to the late sister of the 1
st
Respondent. During the life of the late Mrs. ‘Mapheello Sepamo (Nee ‘Masechaba Tšoaeli), the mother of the 1
st
Respondent and grandmother to the 2
nd
Respondent, she bequeathed her residential property to both the 1
st
and 2
nd
Respondent. The said property or part thereof, which is situated at Ha Fako, in the district of Maseru, is the subject of this matter.
The 2
nd
Respondent got married and now lives in the Botha-Bothe district while the 1
st
Respondent still lives in the property in question. Realising that the house in question has deteriorated, and that her uncle needed some other household property, the 2
nd
Respondent agreed with her uncle that they should subdivide the site and sell its portion thereof to fund the renovation of the house and buy other household property. It is as a result of this idea that the Applicant and the Respondents entered into deliberations for the Applicant to buy the portion of the site in question from the Respondents.
The parties ended up being at loggerheads about whether there was a contract or not. It is as a result of this dispute that the Applicant instituted the present proceedings on the 24
th
day of September, 2024. He claimed the following reliefs;
Cancellation of the agreement of sale between the parties;
Payment of M47, 000.00;
Payment of 15 percent per annum interest on the above amount from date of judgment;
Costs of suit at an attorney and client scale
Any further and/or alternative relief.
[C] THE APPLICANT’S CASE
The Applicant’s first witness (AW1) was one ‘Matšepang Sepetla. She testified that she works as an estate agent. She testified that the agreement between the parties started by the 2
nd
Respondent calling her and asking her to advertise the sale of a portion of her site that she owned jointly with the 1
st
Respondent for sale. She asked her to go to her home, which is the site in issue, meet the 1
st
Respondent and measure the entire plot and the portion to be sold. She went to the property and measured it together with the 1
st
Respondent.
AW1 testified further that she measured the site together with the 1
st
Respondent. A toilet, which was a standalone structure, formed part of the portion to be sold and the 1
st
Respondent had no problem with that, and that the Applicant could build him his own toilet. On the 23
rd
day of April, 2019, the Applicant came to pay for the site. She called the 1
st
Respondent and informed him that Applicant had come to pay, and the 1
st
Respondent said he had just got a temporary job and could not come. They continued with the transaction with 2
nd
Respondent and reduced that transaction to writing before Chief Fako Makhoathi.
The Applicant pleaded and testified as the Second Applicant’s Witness (AW2) that on the 23
rd
day of April, 2019, he entered into a partly verbal, partly written contract with both Respondents for the sale of rights and title to a portion of land numbered Plot Number 14302-160. The said portion measured 30 x 20 square metres and the purchase price was Forty-seven thousand Maloti (M47, 000.00). He said he had got wind of the sale of the said site from one ‘Matšepang Sepetla (AW1). On that day, the 23
rd
day of April, 2019, he met both Respondents. It became apparent under cross examination that when he went to pay, the 1
st
Respondent was not there. He was there only when the site was measured. In fulfilment of the payment of the purchase price term, the Applicant paid the said amount to the 2
nd
Respondent (in the absence of the 1
st
Respondent).
[D] THE RESPONDENTS' CASE
The 1
st
Respondent testified as the 1
st
Respondents’ Witness (RW1). Basically, his evidence is that he never met the Applicant until the time he (the Applicant) came to his home and told him that he has bought his home, but he has never agreed to such a transaction. He refuses that he owns the property equally and jointly with the 2
nd
Respondent.
2
nd
Respondent testified as the Respondents’ Witness Two (RW2). She basically supported the Applicant’s case. She testified that she agreed with her uncle (the 1
st
Respondent) that they should sell the portion of the site to fund the renovation of the house and some other household and personal property for 1
st
Applicant such as a fridge and a cellular phone.
Over and above her support of the Applicants’ case, RW2 testified that after receiving the money, they agreed with the 1
st
Respondent that they should go buy the building material to renovate the house. 1
st
Respondent is even the one who called the constructor as well as the electrician on what to buy. He even demolished the part that needed to be demolished on the instruction of the constructor.
RW2 went on to testify that sometime after the works had begun, she got a call from the constructor that Sechaba (1
st
Respondent) was refusing him to work. When she asked him (1
st
Respondent) if that is true, he confirmed that he indeed refused the constructor to work because one neighbour told him that they had sold the entire plot/property and not just a portion thereof.
Advocate Maleke argued that there is no proof that the 1
st
Respondent was ever party to the contract. He cited the cases of
Maphaong v Minister of Education
[1]
,
Pegasus Supplies (Pty) Ltd v Lirahalibonoe (Pty) Ltd
[2]
,
Lesotho National General Insurance v Rhythm and Steal (Pty) Ltd
[3]
and others to argue that there was no meeting of the minds of the parties and therefore no contract. He argues that since the written contract (Exhibit A) does not bear the signature of the 1
st
Defendant, there was no agreement at all. Moreover, he contended that since the agreement was not registered in terms of the
Section 16
of the
Deeds Registry Act
[4]
, there was no agreement.
[E] ANALYSIS
The Respondents pleaded that they deny that there was ever an agreement and denied everything. However, immediately the 2
nd
Respondent supported the case of the Applicant entirely, then their entire defence fell apart. This is not the only aspects that discredit the entire case of the Respondents. The AW1 testified that they measured the site with the 1
st
Respondent, that even on the day that the agreement was reduced to writing, the 1
st
Respondent was called even by the Chief to ascertain that he was party to the agreement, and he agreed that he was party to the agreement. This evidence is corroborated not only by the Applicant but even by the 2
nd
Respondent. When this is taken together with the fact that the 1
st
Respondent ends up even denying that the site was bequeathed to both of them, it is more believable. The fact that the 1
st
Respondent denied such an obvious fact, painted him as a person who was adamant on denying everything, even the obviously clear fact at all costs.
In ascertaining if a contract exists or if there was a meeting of the minds, all the surrounding facts have to be looked at. In
Pegasus
[5]
, I quoted with approval the words of Howard J in the South African case of
Allen v Sixteen Stirling Investment (Pty) Ltd
.
[6]
I will once again quote the said words verbatim as they illuminate my reasoning herein. They read thus:
“Numerous cases were cited, and counsel debated at length on the question whether the true basis of contract in our law is subjective (the theory of ‘consequentiality’ or objective (the ‘reliance’ theory). I accept that our law follows a generally objective approach to the creation or existence of contracts…but I cannot accept that this approach is so uncompromising that it precludes the plaintiff from advancing the cause of action which he has pleaded [mutual error in corpore]. In Trollip v Jordaan Steyn CJ considered a series of decisions relevant to this question, including the Potato Board case and South African Railways & Harbours v National Bank of South Africa Ltd and held, in effect, that the objective approach to contracts did not exclude the operation, according to the established principles of our law, of mistake as a ground for avoiding contractual liability. I do not think that the majority judgment in Trollip v Jordaan reveals any disagreement with the conclusion of Steyn CJ on this point.”
The fact that the 1
st
Respondent has measured the site with Applicant and AW1, introduced Applicant to a neighbour as the owner of the sub-divided portion of the site, participated in the renovation of the house funded with the proceeds of the sale of the site and even accepted the cellular phone and the fridge are all the surrounding circumstances that support the version that he was party to the agreement. That he is now reneging and no longer wants to see it through, is prove that he has not performed his term of the contract.
Advocate Maleke argued that this is a case in which the order for cost is unwarranted since the 1
st
Applicant was only defending his rights. I do not agree, while I accept that the costs should be as guided by
Section 10
of the
Legal Aid Act
[7]
, this is a case that would warrant the granting of costs. There was a time that I even wondered if the 1
st
Respondent had enough comprehension of the case due to his adamant manner of denying the obvious. However, I concluded that his strong headedness is what is driving him and not his lack of comprehension. It is for the aforesaid reasons that I make the following order:
ORDER
The agreement is cancelled as prayed;
Respondents to pay M47, 000.00 to the Applicant jointly and severally;
Respondents to pay interest on the above amount at the rate of 7.5% from the date of judgment; And,
Costs of suit per the Legal Aid Act.
_______________________
M.S. Kopo
Judge of the High Court
For Applicant: ADV. LETUKA KW
For Respondents: ADV. MALEKE LF
